title: Transactions
objectives: Choose isolation levels; understand the anomalies each prevents

sections.markdown: A transaction is a group of operations that succeed or fail together. The **ACID** properties — Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — guarantee correctness even under concurrency and crashes.

The classic transfer example proves atomicity: without a transaction, a crash between the two updates leaves money missing. Combined with isolation, you also avoid seeing half-applied transfers from other users.
Keeping transactions open across UI clicks or API calls. Long transactions block others, hold locks, and (in Postgres) prevent VACUUM. Keep transactions small and fast.
Retry on deadlocks (they're expected under contention). Use SELECT FOR UPDATE only when you actually need to lock rows. In distributed systems, prefer sagas or the Outbox pattern to two-phase commit.

flashcards.back: Dirty reads and non-repeatable reads; may still allow phantoms in some engines.
Detect + retry with backoff; acquire locks in a consistent order to reduce chance.

quiz.explain: Either all operations commit or none do.
It gives the illusion of a serial schedule.

quiz.q: The right response to a deadlock is…
quiz.explain: Deadlocks are expected under contention; retry the transaction.

challenges.text: Wrap a transfer between two accounts in a transaction.
Reproduce a lost-update anomaly, then fix with SELECT FOR UPDATE.
Trigger and observe a deadlock; fix with consistent lock ordering + retry.
Implement a Saga across two services with compensating transactions.

interviewQuestions: Isolation levels and the anomalies each prevents?
Why avoid distributed transactions in microservices?
